turns.text: Hello Jessica, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I honestly imagined someone who knew me — one midwife or provider who’d remember my story, my worries about being forty-two, the swelling I’d been tracking, and the blur of that last scary week when my vision went. I thought they’d be the same calm face each visit, someone who could read my chart without me having to re-tell the worst parts and who’d reassure me when a headache spiked my panic.

Instead it’s been rotating clinicians and specialists, especially once I was labeled high-risk. Every appointment feels like starting over: new face, new questions, me trying to compress my whole medical life into five minutes. Technically they’re good, but that continuity I craved just isn’t there. Once in awhile a nurse remembers a detail and I’m relieved, but mostly I feel like a set of readings to be managed, not a frightened mother. That lack of a steady, known provider makes the distance from Rockland feel even longer and my late-night worries louder.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

Before labour, what did you expect creating and using a birth plan to be like — how it would be received by staff and how much it would guide your care? And what actually happened with your birth plan during labour?

Before labour, what did you expect your pain management options to be — the range available, how they'd be offered, and how you'd be supported in deciding? And what was the reality of pain management during your labour?

When you arrived at the birth setting — whether hospital, birth centre, or home — what did you expect it to feel like in terms of safety, comfort, and how you'd be welcomed? And what was your actual experience of the environment when you arrived?

If your birth involved an unexpected intervention or complication, what did you expect the process of being informed and involved in those decisions to look like? And how were those decisions actually made and communicated in the moment?

I honestly expected, if something went wrong, that someone would pull me aside, explain calmly what was happening, give me options and a little time to breathe and ask questions. I kept imagining a nurse or my midwife kneeling down at eye level, saying “we need to do X because of Y,” and letting me and my husband be part of the decision—not just sign forms while everything moves around us.

The reality was harsher. When my blood pressure spiked and the team decided it was time to act, it felt urgent—because it was—but it also felt rushed and clinical. I was told what would happen in clipped sentences, sometimes with medical terms that landed like a wall. There were moments they checked in with me, but often choices were presented as the only safe path. I get why they had to move fast, and I’m grateful they did, but I left feeling sidelined—like someone had acted on my body without fully bringing me into the conversation. That still stings.

During labour, what did you expect the midwife or doctor present to provide in terms of emotional support and personal attention — not just clinical care? And what did you actually experience from the staff who were with you?

Before birth, what did you expect the immediate period after your baby was born to be like — skin-to-skin contact, who would be present, what would be done? And what actually happened in those first moments after delivery?

I guess I keep circling back to feeling unseen. Clinically, people do their jobs well — they catch the blood pressure spikes, they explain the risks in technical terms — but emotionally I’m left raw and alone. I replay that Tuesday when my vision blurred: the panic, the ambulance ride, the thin voice in a gown trying to make sense of “high-risk” while the nurse scribbled numbers. My husband held my hand, but once he went back to work it’s just the house, the wallpaper in the nursery I stare at instead of sleeping, and the constant question: will I get to bring this baby home?

I want practical things: a single provider who remembers me, clearer explanations in plain language, someone to call on sleepless nights who actually listens. I want to be treated like a mother, not a case file. It’s hard to balance gratitude for competent care with the grief for the kind of experience I’d hoped for. Still, I try to hold on to the small comforts — my husband’s text messages, the moments of gentle kindness from a nurse — and the stubborn hope that we’ll get through this.
Were there moments when a doctor, partner, or family member's opinion overrode yours — even when you hadn't asked for their input?

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?
